title: Making Subjects and Verbs Agree: A Parent's Guide
whatIsIt: Subject-verb agreement means that the verb in a sentence must match the subject in number. If the subject is singular (one person or thing), the verb usually gets an -s. If the subject is plural (more than one), the verb stays in its base form. This rule seems small, but it's one of the most important patterns in English — it appears in almost every sentence!
howToHelp: Play the 'One or Many?' game — point to objects around the house and say both versions: 'The cat sleeps' versus 'The cats sleep'. Make it silly and fun!
Use hand signals: hold up one finger for singular subjects (add -s!) and multiple fingers for plural subjects (no -s!). Physical movement helps memory stick.
When reading together, pause at verbs and ask: 'How many people are doing this action?' This builds the habit of checking agreement automatically.
Create agreement flashcards with pictures — one dog / two dogs, one child / two children — and practice the matching verb forms.
Sing songs that emphasize the -s sound: 'She walks, he walks, it walks, but they walk, we walk, you walk!' Rhythm makes grammar memorable.
Point out that 'you' is special — it works with plural verbs even when talking to one person. English has a few delightful exceptions!
commonMistakes: 'She go to school' instead of 'She goes' — forgetting the -s for he/she/it is the most common agreement error in early learning.
'The dogs barks' instead of 'The dogs bark' — adding -s to plural verbs is a natural overcorrection.
'Everyone are happy' instead of 'Everyone is happy' — words like everyone, someone, and anybody feel plural but are grammatically singular.

'The group of students are loud' instead of 'The group ... is loud' — when a phrase comes between the subject and verb, children sometimes match the verb to the nearest noun instead of the real subject.
signsOfProgress: Automatically adds -s for he/she/it in simple present sentences without thinking about it.
Notices agreement errors in other people's speech or in written text — this shows the rule is becoming automatic.
Can explain WHY a verb needs -s: 'Because it's just one person, Mom!' — verbalizing rules shows deep understanding.
Handles tricky subjects like 'everyone' and 'nobody' with increasing confidence.
Self-corrects agreement mistakes in real-time conversation — the ultimate sign of mastery.
ifStruggling: Go back to the basics — practice only with pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they) until those feel automatic.
Use color-coding: highlight singular subjects in one color and plural subjects in another. Visual patterns build intuition.
Don't worry about collective nouns or intervening phrases yet — those come after the core pattern is solid.
Read aloud together and exaggerate the -s sound: 'She gooooes!' — silliness reduces anxiety and increases retention.
Remember that this rule takes months (sometimes years) to fully internalize. Native English-speaking children also struggle with it.
Praise effort over perfection: 'I love how you thought about whether it was one or many!'
